Introduction and Review of
Probability Theory Course Goals:
• Review a very broad collection of methods so you are
introduced to the most fundamental statistical
procedures.
• Provide you with both theoretical and practical
background, including real data examples.
• Understand the assumptions and limitations of
statistical methods.
• Being able to select the appropriate method for
analyzing a specific dataset.

What is Statistics?
• A branch of mathematics that learns from data
• Pause: Then what is machine learning??
• Applied statistics: concerned with methods for collecting,
analyzing, and drawing conclusions from data.
• Theoretical statistics: establishes the properties and conditions
under which the data analysis methods work.
• Without rudimentary knowledge of how it works, people can't
make evaluations of a wide variety of numerical encounters in
daily life.
• Many statistical methods are relevant and useful in a wide
range of academic and non-academic disciplines, beyond just
the statistical and data sciences.

Example
Data collected in 19th century by Darwin who studied
the effect of pollination on Zea mays plants (aka maize
or corn).
See 01-Introduction.R file
pot pair type height
1 I a cross 23.500
2 I a self 17.375
3 I b cross 12.000
4 I b self 20.375
5 I c cross 21.000
6 I c self 20.000
7 II d cross 22.000
8 II d self 20.000
9 II e cross 19.125
10 II e self 18.375
…

Research question:
Do cross-fertilized plants tend to grow taller?
How do we answer this
question scientifically?
• You can examine the
difference between the
two groups visually.
• Or you can write a
probabilistic model
using random variables
and then test a
statistical hypothesis.

Perform a hypothesis test:
> t.test(d2$diff)
One Sample t-test
data: d2$diff
t = 2.148, df = 14, p-value = 0.0497
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
0.003899165 5.229434169
sample estimates:
mean of x
2.616667
What do these results mean and how was this test developed?

Key idea in statistics:
explain the variation
• To do this we need to express scientifically the variation in the data
as observations of random variables and use a statistical model to
explain it (at least partially). Statistical analysis requires data,
which may be viewed as an observed sample from a population.
• The data X , … , X are considered as n, usually independent and
1 n
identically distributed, random variables with a common
population distribution/model. This population can be specified as
a formula with either its cumulative distribution function (cdf) F(x)
or its probability density function (pdf) f(x).

Back to the maize example
• Let a typical height of the self-pollinated plant be denoted Y
• Let a typical height of the cross-pollinated plant be denoted X
• Then one possible model is:
𝑌 = 𝜇 + 𝜎𝜀
𝑋 = 𝜇 + 𝜂 + 𝜎𝜀
• 𝜇, 𝜂, and 𝜎 have unknown values called model parameters. They
need to be learned from the data.
• 𝜀 is a random variable, called error or noise
• The research question is now: can we demonstrate that 𝜂 > 0?
• There are always assumptions made to answer the research
question. For example, 𝜀 ~ 𝑁(0,1).

Back to the maize example (continued):
• In the context of a sample from a population, X and Y will
have indexes to indicate that.
• Also, it is very common to use indexes for the different
groups (in this case two groups: self and cross-pollinated).
• Then a more rigorously written model would be:
𝑌 = 𝜇 + 𝜎𝜀 , 𝑖 = 1, … , 𝑛
𝑖 1 1𝑖 1
𝑋 = 𝜇 + 𝜎𝜀 , 𝑖 = 1, … , 𝑛
𝑖 2 2𝑖 2
Research question would be: can we show that 𝜇 > 𝜇 ?
2 1
There are more details: Darwin controlled the effect of
temperature and humidity by planting in the same pot
several pairs of cross-pollinated and self-pollinated plants.
This means we must consider such dependence (see R fil1e8).

• Then to answer the research question we intuitively estimate 𝜂 with
20.19167 − 17.575 = 2.616667
So, the estimated typical difference between cross and self-pollinated
plants is about 2.6 inches and is positive, but …
• How accurate/reliable is this estimate?
• Can we safely conclude it is positive not by random chance, but due
to systematic difference between the two groups? 19

Some Answers
– To ”prove” there is a systematic difference, we
need to formalize the model and then use
probability theory results.
– The answer will clearly depend on the sample size
n and is usually more reliable as n increases.
– The assumptions play a key role in the
trustworthiness of the result. Are the sample
observations independent and identically
distributed (iid); is the population normally
distributed; …

Review of Important
Probability Theory Results Random Sample
• The observed sample is a collection of numbers, also
known as the observations, the data or the data set.
These are represented using lowercase letters, for
example, x , … , x .
1 n
• The random sample is a collection of random variables
whose properties form our model for the data.
Let X , … , X be iid random sample from a population
1 n

This means:
1. Each X , i = 1, … , n is a random variable.
i

i

STAT 5703
Statistical Inference and Modeling
for Data Science
Dobrin Marchev

Estimation
Lecture Goals:
Define
estimators
Study properties
of estimators Parametric vs. Nonparametric Models
• Our data are X , … , X which are iid random sample from a
1 n
population with a pdf f(x) or cdf F(x).
• Very often the population distribution is not completely known,
and f(x) depends on an unknown parameter 𝜃, which can be
indicated with f(x; 𝜃).
• When f(x) depends on an unknown parameter 𝜃, we say that we
are considering a parametric model for our data.
• Example: linear regression is a parametric model.
• Non-parametric methods are statistical techniques that do not rely
on specific assumptions about the underlying distribution of the
population being studied.
• Example: K-Nearest Neighbor is a nonparametric model.
• In this course we focus on the parametric models.

Goal of Estimation
We assume we know the formula for f(x; θ), except for the
value of θ, which is an unknown parameter.
Assume θ is identifiable, that is,
if θ ≠ θ , then f(x; θ ) ≠ f(x; θ )
1 2 1 2

Interpretation
Q: What does 95% confidence mean?
A: The procedure we are using to construct the interval
is 95% reliable. That is, 95% of all the intervals
constructed with this method will cover the unknown
value of 𝜇, and 5% will miss it.
Therefore, we don’t say 𝜇 ∈ (l, u) with probability
95%, because 𝜇 is not a random variable! The best way
express the interpretation is to say:
We are 95% confident that 𝜇 is between l and u.

Interpretation Note on the width of the CI
The width of the confidence interval, that is, its
accuracy depends on:
• The sample size n: the larger the sample size the
narrow the width of the CI.
• The confidence level: the higher the confidence the
wider the CI will be!
• The standard deviation of the population or SE: the
larger the SE the wider the CI will be.
• The method used to construct the CI

Resampling Methods
Module 3:
Resampling Methods in Machine Learning
Reading:
Chapter 5 from
“An Introduction to Statistical Learning”
Lecturer: Nathan Bastian, Section: XXX

Lesson Goals
Understand the basic concepts of resampling methods for
▪
model selection and assessment.
Understand the similarities and differences between the
▪
validation set approach, leave-one-out cross-validation, and
K-fold cross-validation.
Understand the basic theoretical concepts of the bootstrap
▪
method for assessing statistical accuracy.
Recognize that the performance of machine learning models
▪
depends on their prediction capability on independent test
data.

Resampling Methods
Involves repeatedly drawing samples from a training set and
▪
refitting a model or estimator of interest on each sample to obtain
more information about the fitted model/estimator.
Example: We can estimate the variability of a linear regression fit
▪
by repeatedly drawing different samples from the training data,
fitting a OLS regression to each new sample, and then examining
the extent to which the resulting fits differ.
Model Assessment: having chosen a final model, estimating its
▪
prediction error on new data.
Model Selection: estimating the performance of different models
▪
in order to choose the best one.

Resampling Methods (cont.)
Cross-Validation
▪
– Used to estimate test set prediction error rates
associated with a given statistical model, or to
select the appropriate level of model complexity.
Bootstrap
▪
– Used mostly to provide a measure of accuracy of
a parameter estimate or of a given statistical
method.

Model Assessment
Test Error
▪
– The average error that results from using a statistical method to
predict the response on a new observation.
– The prediction error over an independent test sample.
Training Error
▪
– The average loss over the training sample:
𝑁

መ
෍ 𝐿[𝑦 , 𝑓 𝑥 ]
𝑖 𝑖
𝑁
𝑖=1
Note: The training error rate can dramatically underestimate the
▪
test error rate

Model Assessment (cont.)
As the model becomes more and more
▪
complex, it uses the training data more
and can adapt to more complicated
underlying structures.
Hence, there is a decrease in bias but
▪
an increase in variance.
However, training error is not a good
▪
estimate of the test error.
• Training error consistently decreases with model complexity.
• A model with zero training error is overfit to the training data and
will typically generalize poorly.

Model Assessment (cont.)
If we are in a data-rich situation, the best approach for both model
▪
selection and model assessment is to randomly divide the dataset
into three parts: training set, validation set, and test set.
The training set is used to fit the models. The validation set is used
▪
to estimate prediction error for model selection. The test set is used
for assessment of the prediction error of the final chosen model.
A typical split might by 50% for training, and 25% each for
▪
validation and testing.

Model Assessment (cont.)
Best solution: use a large designated test set, which is often not available.
▪
For the methods presented here, there is insufficient data to split it into
three parts.
There are some methods to make mathematical adjustments to the training
▪
error rate in order to estimate the test error rate:
– C statistic, AIC, BIC (we will discuss these next week)
p
Here, we consider cross-validation (CV) methods that estimate the test
▪
error by holding out a subset of the training observations from the fitting
process, and then applying statistical method to those held out observations.
For example, leave out 1/10 of subjects, develop model in the remaining
▪
9/10, evaluate in the withheld 1/10, repeat 10 times and average (so called
10-fold CV)
Still not very precise way to measure accuracy
▪

Overview: Model Selection
By far, the most important use of
▪
validation is for model selection.
Suppose we have M models;
▪
validation can be used to select one of
these models.
We use the training data to fit the
▪
model, and we evaluate each model
on the validation set to obtain the
validation errors.
It is now a simple matter to select the
▪
model with the lowest validation
error.

Validation Set Approach
Suppose that we would like to find a set of variables that give the
▪
lowest validation error rate (an estimate of the test error rate).
If we have a large data set, we can achieve this goal by randomly
▪
splitting the data into separate training and validation data sets.
Then, we use the training data set to build each possible model and
▪
select the model that gives the lowest error rate when applied to the
validation data set.
Training Data Validation Data

Model Selection via Validation Set
set.seed(3249)
x <- runif(100, -3, 3)
y <- 2 + 2*x - 1*x^2 + .5*x^3 + rnorm(100, mean = 1, sd = 4)
dat <- data.frame(cbind(x, y))
plot(dat)
curve(expr = 2 + 2*x - 1*x^2 + .5*x^3,
from = -3, to = 3, n = 501, add = TRUE,
lwd = 3, col = 4)
Generate data (100 cases)
from a cubic model with
normal errors. There is one
outcome y and one
predictor x.

### Randomly select 1/2 of cases for a test set

Problems With a Single Validation
Set
Beyond the variability identified in the last plot, another concern is that
▪
only a subset of the observations are used in the model training.
In our case, we used 50 instead of 100 observations to fit the model.
▪
Most statistical methods perform more poorly when they are trained on
▪
fewer observations, so use of a single validation set may lead to
overestimation of the error rate.
Cross-validation is an extension of the validation set approach we just
▪
discussed that can address both issues:
1. Cross-validation averages error rates over multiple samples (i.e.,
resampling) to address the variability of MSEs.
2. Cross-validation fits may be carried out with as many as all but
one of the observations in a data set, thereby minimizing the
extent to which we overestimate error rate due to lack of sufficient
sample size relative to the full sample.

Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
Instead of creating two subsets of comparable size, a single
▪
observation is used for the validation set and the remaining
observations (n – 1) make up the training set.
LOOCV Algorithm:
▪
– Split the entire data set of size n into:
• Blue = training data set
• Beige = validation data set
– Fit the model using the training data set
– Evaluate the model using validation set and
compute the corresponding MSE.
𝑛

– Repeat this process n times, producing n
CV = ෍ MSE
(𝑛) 𝑖 squared errors. The average of these n squared
𝑛
errors estimates the test MSE.
𝑖=1

LOOCV MSEs
### LOOCV with package boot
library(boot)
### LOOCV function for polynomial fits
looPoly <- function(deg, dat) {
lm1 <- glm(y ~ poly(x, degree = deg, raw = TRUE),
family = "gaussian", data = dat)
out <- cv.glm(data = dat, glmfit = lm1, K = nrow(dat))$delta[1]
out
}
looOut <- sapply(1:20, FUN = looPoly, dat = dat)
LOOCV correctly identifies degree = 3 as having the
lowest MSE, though degrees 3 through 15 are similar.

Validation Set Approach vs. LOOCV
LOOCV has far less bias and, therefore, tends not to overestimate
▪
the test error rate.
Performing LOOCV multiple times always yields the same results
▪
because there is no randomness in the training/validation set splits.
LOOCV is computationally intensive because the model has to be
▪
fit n times. However, there is a shortcut with OLS linear or
polynomial regression (where h is the leverage):

K-Fold Cross-Validation
Probably the simplest and most widely used method for estimating
▪
prediction error.
This method directly estimates the average prediction error when the
▪
machine learning method is applied to an independent test sample.
Ideally, if we had enough data, we would set aside a validation set (as
▪
previously described) and use it to assess the performance of our
prediction model.
To finesse the problem, K-fold cross-validation uses part of the available
▪
data to fit the model, and a different part to test it.

K-Fold Cross-Validation (cont.)
We use this method because LOOCV is
▪
computationally intensive/slow.
We randomly divide the data set of into
▪
K folds (typically K = 5 or 10).
One of the folds is treated as a test set, and the method is fit on the
▪
remaining K – 1 folds. The MSPE is computed on the observations
in the held-out fold. The process is repeated K times, taking out a
different part each time.
By averaging the K estimates of the test error, we get an estimated
▪
validation (test) error rate for new observations.

K-Fold Cross-Validation (cont.)
Let the K folds be C , … , C , where C denotes the indices of the
▪
1 K k

k

Bias-Variance Trade-off for K-Fold
Cross-Validation
Which is better, LOOCV or K-fold CV?
▪
– LOOCV is more computationally intensive than K-fold CV
– From the perspective of bias reduction, LOOCV is preferred to K-fold CV (when K < n)
– However, LOOCV has higher variance than K-fold CV (when K < n)
– Thus, we see the bias-variance trade-off between the two resampling methods
We tend to use K-fold CV with K = 5 or K = 10, as these values have been
▪
shown empirically to yield test error rate estimates that suffer neither from
excessively high bias nor from very high variance

use MSE to quantify test error, we instead use the number of
misclassified observation.

The Bootstrap
The bootstrap is a flexible and powerful statistical tool that
▪
can be used to quantify uncertainty associated with a given
estimator or statistical method; it is a general tool for
assessing statistical accuracy.
As an example, the bootstrap can be used to estimate the
▪
standard errors of the coefficients from a linear regression
fit, or a confidence interval for that coefficient.
The use of the term bootstrap derives from the phrase “to
▪
pull oneself up by one’s bootstraps.”

The Bootstrap: Overview The Bootstrap: Overview (cont.)
Suppose we have a model fit to a set of training data. We denote
▪
the training set by Z = (z , z , . . . , z ) where z = (x , y ).
1 2 N i i i
The basic idea is to randomly draw datasets with replacement
▪
from the training data, each sample the same size as the original
training set.
This is done B times, producing B bootstrap datasets. Then we refit
▪
the model to each of the bootstrap datasets, and examine the
behavior of the fits over the B replications.

The Bootstrap: Overview (cont.)
S(Z) is any quantity computed from
▪
the data Z, for example, the
prediction at some input point.
From the bootstrap sampling we can
▪
estimate any aspect of the
distribution of S(Z), for example, its
variance:
Note that this estimated variance can be
thought of as a Monte-Carlo estimate of
the variance of S(Z) under sampling
from the empirical distribution function.

The Bootstrap: An Example
Suppose that we wish to invest a fixed sum of money in two
▪
financial assets that yield returns X and Y; note that X and Y are
random variables.
We will invest a fraction α of our money in X, and will invest
▪
the remaining 1 – α in Y.
Goal: Since there is variability associated with the returns on
▪
these two assets, we wish to choose α to minimize the total
risk, or variance, of our investment.

The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)
In other words, we want to minimize Var(αX + (1 – α)Y)
▪
The value that minimizes the risk, in this examples, is:

The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)
We can use this approach for estimating α on a simulated data
▪

In order to quantify the accuracy of our estimate of α, we also

The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)
In rough terms, for a random sample from the population, we would

In the real world, this procedure cannot be applied because for real
▪
data we cannot generate new samples from the original population.
The bootstrap approach, however, allows us to use a computer to
▪
mimic the process of obtaining new data sets; this enables use to
estimate the variability of our estimate without generating
additional samples.

The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)
Rather than repeatedly obtaining independent data
▪
sets from the population, we instead obtain
distinct data sets by repeatedly sampling
observations from the original data set with
replacement.
Each of these bootstrap data sets is created by
▪
sampling with replacement, and is the same size
as the original data set.
As a result, some observations may appear more
▪
than once in a given bootstrap data set and some
not at all.

The Bootstrap: More Details
Although the bootstrap is used primarily to obtain standard errors of
▪
an estimate, it can also provide approximate confidence intervals
for a population parameters.
– One approach is known as Bootstrap Percentile confidence interval.
In cross-validation, each of the K validation folds is distinct from
▪
the other K – 1 folds used for training (i.e. there is no overlap).
To estimate the prediction error using the bootstrap, one approach
▪
would be to fit the model in question on a set of bootstrap samples,
and then keep track of how well it predicts the original training set.

Hypothesis Tests
Now that we’ve studied confidence intervals and point
estimators, let’s study another commonly used method for
statistical inference: hypothesis testing.

Motivating example:
Does gender affect promotions at a bank?
• Say you are working at a bank in the 1970s and you are submitting your
résumé to apply for a promotion. Will your gender affect your chances of
getting promoted?
• To answer this question, we’ll focus on data from a study published in the
Journal of Applied Psychology in 1974.
• To begin the study, 48 bank supervisors were asked to assume the role
of a hypothetical director of a bank with multiple branches. Every one of
the bank supervisors was given a résumé and asked whether the
candidate on the résumé was fit to be promoted to a new position in one
of their branches.
• However, each of these 48 résumés were identical in all respects except
one: the name of the applicant at the top of the résumé. Of the
supervisors, 24 were randomly given résumés with stereotypically “male”
names, while 24 of the supervisors were randomly given résumés with
stereotypically “female” names.

Q: Does this provide

is gender discrimination in
promotions at banks?
A: One way to test this
hypothesis is by reshuffling the
sample.

Motivating example:
Does gender affect promotions at a bank?
• Try to imagine a hypothetical universe
where no gender discrimination in
promotions existed. In such a
hypothetical universe, the gender of an
applicant would have no bearing on
their chances of promotion.
• Bringing things back to our promotions
data frame, the gender variable would
thus be an irrelevant label.
• If these gender labels were irrelevant,
then we could randomly reassign them
by “shuffling” them to no consequence!
• It appears the difference in “male
names” versus “female names”
promotion rates is now different.

Motivating example:
Does gender affect promotions at a bank?
• The final step is to measure how
surprised we are by a promotion
difference of 29.2% in a
hypothesized universe of no
gender discrimination. If the
observed difference of 0.292 is
highly unlikely, then we would
be inclined to reject the validity
of our hypothesized universe.
• This is known as a permutation
test. You can use it to quantify
the probability the original
labeling was random or not by
reshuffling many times.
• It is a nonparametric method. In
this course we focus on
parametric hypothesis testing.

Definition
• Hypothesis tests allow us to take a sample of data from a
population and infer about the plausibility of competing
hypotheses.
• A hypothesis is a claim about the value of the unknown
population parameter.
• For example:

P-value
Definition: The p-value of a hypothesis test is the
probability of obtaining a value of the test statistic which is
more extreme than the one observed in the current sample,
assuming H is true.

More extreme means more contradictory to H

Let X be the test statistic (as r.v./formula) and x be its
observed value (when calculated from the sample). Then:
• If right-tail H : 𝜃 > 𝜃 , then p-value = P(X > x | H )
a 0 0
• If left-tail H : 𝜃 < 𝜃 , then p-value = P(X < x | H )
a 0 0
• If two-tail H : 𝜃 ≠ 𝜃 , then p-value = P(|X| > |x| | H )
a 0 0

Example 3 (cont.): Find the p-value
Conclusion:
Since p-value = 0.0808 < 0.1 = 𝛼,
we reject H

How do we use the p–value?
Fundamental Rule of Statistic:
Reject H if p-value < 𝛼

That’s why the p-value is aka the lowest 𝛼
level at which H can be rejected.

Example 7:
A movie theater owner likes to keep a very close eye on
how much popcorn goes into each bag sold, so he
knows that the amount is normally distributed and the
average bag has 8 cups of popcorn and that this varies a
little bit, about half a cup. That is, the population mean
should be μ = 8.00 and the known population standard
deviation is σ = 0.50. The owner wants to make sure
that the newest employee is filling bags correctly, so
over the course of a week he randomly assesses 25 bags
filled by the employee to test for a difference (n = 25).
He doesn’t want bags overfilled or under filled, so he
looks for differences in both directions.

Aside: KL divergence
One way to measure the dissimilarity between two
probability distributions, p and q, is known as the
Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL divergence) or relative
entropy. This is defined as follows:
𝑞 𝑥
KL 𝑞 𝑝 = න 𝑞 𝑥 log 𝑑𝑥
𝑝(𝑥)
Typically, q(x) represents the “true” distribution of the data.
The pdf p(x) then represents a theory, model, description,
or approximation of q(x), and KL 𝑞 𝑝 measures how close
is p to q.
Note: KL is not a proper distance, because it is not
symmetric and does not satisfy the triangular inequality.

Permutation Test Example
We use the chickwts data in R. Weights in grams are
recorded, for six groups of newly hatched chicks fed
different supplements for 6 weeks. There are six types of
feed supplements.

Permutation Test Example
These are the soybean and linseed groups:
> x
[1] 158 171 193 199 230 243 248 248 250 267 271 316 327 329
> y
[1] 141 148 169 181 203 213 229 244 257 260 271 309
The groups can be compared in several ways. For
example, sample means difference: 27.67857
or sample medians difference: 27
More generally, one can ask whether the distributions of
the two variables differ and compare the groups by any
statistic that measures a distance between two samples.
We will use the two-sample t-test as a metric.

Permutation Test Example
If the two samples are drawn from normal populations with
equal variances, we can apply the two-sample t-test:
t.test(x, y, var.equal = T)
Two Sample t-test
data: x and y
t = 1.3208, df = 24, p-value = 0.199
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
-15.57282 70.92996
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y
246.4286 218.7500

Permutation Test Example
• Assume, however, the distributions of the weights are
unknown.
• The achieved significance level of the test statistic can be
computed from the permutation distribution without
requiring distributional assumptions.
• If the diets are identical, any split of the combined
sample into m = 14 and n = 12 subgroups should result in
similar group means.
• We will recalculate the value of the test statistic on many
different permutations and evaluate how likely the
observed t = 1.32 is.
• This way, we obtain the so-called empirical p-value.

Permutation Test Example
The approximate permutation test procedure is illustrated
below with the two-sample t statistic.
R = 999 # number of replications of the test statistic
z = c(x, y) # vectorized data from the two samples
K <- 1:26
reps <- numeric(R); t0 <- t.test(x, y, var.equal = T)$statistic
#number of replicates
#pooled sample
#storage for replicates
for (i in 1:R) {
#generate indices k for the first sample
k <- sample(K, size = 14, replace = FALSE)
x1 <- z[k]
y1 <- z[-k] #complement of x1
reps[i] <- t.test(x1, y1, var.equal = T)$statistic
}
p <- mean(c(t0, reps) >= t0)
> p
[1] 0.103

Permutation Test Example
Since we are doing a two-tail test the empirical p-value is
2*(0.103) = 0.206 so the null hypothesis is not rejected. For
comparison, the two-sample t-test reports p-value = 0.198.
(The normality tests do not reject the null hypothesis so
perhaps the t-test was ok)

Wishart Distribution
• The Chi-square random variable with n degrees of freedom is
a sum of squares of n independent standard normal variables.
• The Wishart distribution is a sum of outer products of normal
random vectors.
• It is a random matrix which is symmetric and positive definite.
• Let X , … , X ~ N (𝜇, 𝛴). Then the distribution of the p×p
1 n p

distribution with df = n.

• Notation: 𝑴 ~ 𝑊 (𝚺, 𝑛)

Example:
Does Waffle House cause?
11-13 Correlation is
commonplace
• http://www.tylervigen.com/spurious-correlations
• AI explanation:
As butter consumption in Colorado increased, so did the
demand for larger sample sizes in epidemiological studies.
This led to a surge in funding for public health research,
prompting a migration of epidemiologists to the state, lured
by the opportunity to conduct margarineally significant
studies and spread their knowledge like, well, butter.
How this works
Data dredging: They have 25,153 variables in a database. They compare all these
variables against each other to find ones that randomly match up. That's 632,673,409
correlation calculations! This is called “data dredging.”

Common Elemental Confounds
Sometimes factors other than the IV may influence the DV in an experiment. These
unwanted influences are called confounding variables.

11-17 Categorical from Continuous Predictors
• Sometimes you might want to create a categorical
predictor from a continuous.
• For example, instead of exact amount of income, you
may want to split it to just three cases: low-, middle-
and high-income categories.
• Why?
• Such model can handle non-linear relationships and
provides more flexibility.
• These models are known as step-function or regression
tree models.
11-18

Moving Beyond Linearity
• The linearity assumption is good in many statistics problems.
• However, in some cases, the true relationship between the
outcome and a predictor variable might not be linear and as
a result the standard linear regression can have significant
limitations in terms of predictive power.
• There are other methods that offer a lot of flexibility, without
losing the ease and interpretability of the linear models:
– Polynomial regression
– Step functions
– Regression and smoothing splines
– Local regression
– Generalized additive models (GAMs)
11-19

Step Functions
• Using polynomial functions of the
variables as predictor in a linear model
imposes a global structure on the non-
linear function of X. We need more
flexibility for better predictions!
• To avoid imposing such a global
structure, we can create
transformations of a variable by
cutting the variable range into distinct
regions.
• We use step functions to break the
range of X into bins, where we fit a
different constant in each bin.
• Note: the step function regression is
aka regression tree.
11-20

Step Functions
• This amounts to converting a continuous variable into an
ordered categorical variable.
• In greater detail, we create cut-points (or knots) c , c , … , c
1 2 K
in the range of X and then construct K + 1 dummy variables:
where I(∙) is an indicator function that returns a 1 if the
condition is true and 0 otherwise.

0 1 K
since X must be in exactly one of the K + 1 intervals.
• We then use OLS estimation to fit a linear model using these
K + 1 new variables:

𝑖 0 1 1 𝑖 2 2 𝑖 𝐾 𝐾 𝑖 𝑖
• For a given value of X, at most one of C , C , … , C can be
1 2 K
non-zero.
• 𝛽 represents the average increase in the response for X in
𝑗
𝑐 ≤ 𝑋 ≤ 𝑐 relative to X < C .
𝑗 𝑗+1 1
11-22

Tree-Based Methods
• Tree-based methods for regression and classification
involve stratifying or segmenting the predictor space into
several simple regions.
• Since the set of splitting rules used to segment the
predictor space can be summarized in a tree, these
types of machine learning approaches are known as
decision tree methods.
• The basic idea of these methods is to partition the space
and identify some representative centroids.
11-23

Regression Trees
It’s important to note that a single feature can be used multiple times in a
tree:
If we build a deeper tree, we’ll continue to split on the same feature:
11-24

Regression Trees
• In general, we create the partitions by iteratively splitting
one at a time the X variables into two regions.
• First split at X = t
1 1
• If X < t , split at X = t
1 1 2 2
• If X > t , split further at X = t
1 1 1 3
• If X > t , split at X = t
1 3 2 4

Regression Trees (cont.)
• When we create
partitions this way, we
can always represent
them using a tree
structure.
• As a result, this
provides a very simple
way to explain the
model to a non-expert.

Regression Trees (cont.)
• Caution: Without any
controls, a regression
tree will grow
indefinitely and overfit
the data.
• One solution relies on
fitting an ensemble of
trees where each
individual tree is
regularized to be
shallow. This is the
random forests model.

Piecewise Polynomials
• Instead of fitting a high-degree polynomial over the entire
range of X, piecewise polynomial regression involves fitting
separate low-degree polynomials over different regions of X.
• Here the beta coefficients differ in different parts of the range
of X; the points where the coefficients change are called
knots.

Piecewise Polynomials

using the same knots, to obtain a piecewise polynomial fit

parameters

Dobrin Marchev

Regression Model Assumptions
Recall the multiple linear regression model:
Y = X𝛽 + 𝜀

The assumption is that 𝜺 ~ 𝑁 0, 𝜎 𝑰 , which means
𝑛

𝒀|𝑿 ~ 𝑁 X𝛽, 𝜎 𝑰
𝑛
The above implies several things about the errors:
• Normality
• Independence (because uncorrelated and normal)
• Same variance
• The model also implies linearity, in the sense E 𝒀 𝑿 = X𝛽
• How do we check them and what if they are violated?

Non-Normal Errors Detection
• Box-Plot of Residuals – Can be used to check for
symmetry and for outliers
• Check proportion that lie within 2 standard
deviations around 0, ±2*SD, where SD=sqrt(MSE)
• Normal probability plot of residual versus expected
values under normality – should fall approximately
on a straight line (Only works well with moderate to
large samples) qqnorm(e); qqline(e) in R
Expected value of Residuals under Normality:
1) Rank residuals from smallest (large/negative) to highest (large/positive) Rank = k
k −0.375
2) Compute the percentile using p = and obtain corresponding z-value: z(p)
n + 0.25
 
3) Multiply by s = MSE expected residual = MSE z(p)

Tests for Normality of Residuals
• Shapiro-Wilk Test –involves comparing the observed
data with a corresponding set of normally distributed
values. The W statistic is derived from the correlation
between the data and the corresponding normal
scores. A value of W close to 1 suggests that the data
follow a normal distribution. The test is known for its
good power properties, meaning it has a high
probability of correctly detecting departures from
normality even in small samples. In R: use shapiro.test
• Kolmogorov – Smirnov test – a general test for
comparing a sample to any distribution. In R: use
ks.test

What if we drop the normality assumption?
There are several alternatives:
• Assume another distribution (GLM models)
• Transform the data to look “normal”
• Fit a nonparametric regression.
• One example of nonparametric model is the so-called Theil’s
method.
• It still assumes the linearity condition, but normality is replaced
with
• The conditional distribution of Y|x is symmetric and continuous.
• The estimation is done by ranking, like the Wilcoxon test.

Model Departures Detected With Residuals and Plots
• Relation between Y and X is not linear
• Errors have non-constant variance
• Errors are not independent
• Existence of Outlying Observations
• Non-normal Errors
• Missing predictor variable(s)
• Common Plots
▪ Residuals/Absolute Residuals versus Predictor Variable
▪ Residuals/Absolute Residuals versus Predicted Values
▪ Residuals versus Omitted variables
▪ Residuals versus Time
▪ Box Plots, Histograms, Normal Probability Plots

Diagnostics Plots
• The most common diagnostic plot is the residual plot:
Plot (standardized) residuals versus X or residuals vs. predicted values
What to look for?

Polynomial Regression Models
• What is polynomial regression?
– A form of regression analysis in which the relationship between
the independent variables and the dependent variable is modeled
as a polynomial of degree p.
– Fits a nonlinear relationship between x and E(Y | x).
– Usually fit with the method of least squares.

Linear Model Selection
• In some cases, though, there may be good reasons to use other fitting
rules than the normal equations. Why?
– If the sample size n is small and there are a lot of predictors the least
squares fit will have high variability.
– If there are a lot of predictors (i.e., p is large), some of them may be
non-informative. That is, they may not be related to the outcome
variable. In that case, OLS will estimate small coefficients for the
variables but will not set the coefficients to be exactly zero.
– Thus, we might seek out a method that sets the magnitude of some
coefficients to exactly zero. This would result in feature selection or
variable selection.
• Model specification is the process of determining which independent
variables to include and exclude from a regression equation.

Alternatives to OLS for Linear Models
1. Subset selection. Instead of fitting OLS on all possible predictor variables,
we first eliminate some by setting their coefficients to zero and then fit OLS
on the remaining predictors.
2. Regularization/shrinkage. The model is fit on all p predictors, but the
magnitudes of the coefficients are shrunk towards zero. These methods
work by adding a penalty to the loss function based on the magnitude of
the regression coefficients. One method of regularization, called the lasso,
uses SSE plus a regularization term as the loss function:

3. Dimension reduction. Here we project the p predictors down into an M-
dimensional space, where M < p, via linear combinations of variables, or
directions. We then use the smaller set of m projections as the predictors to be
fit by OLS.
4. Partial Least Squares (PLS). A supervised alternative to PCR.

Best Subset Selection
• Algorithm for best subset selection:
1. Let M represent the null model (i.e., the model with no predictors).

This model will only estimate a constant intercept which will
represent the sample average.
2. For k = 1, 2, … , p:
a) Fit all p choose k models that contain exactly k predictors.
b) Select the best-fitting model among all the p choose k models, as
measured by SSE, R2, or some other measure of model fit, and
call it M .

Probabilistic selection: AIC and BIC
• The Akaike Information Criterion, or AIC for short, is a method for scoring and
selecting a model. It is named for the developer of the method, Hirotugu Akaike,
and may be shown to have a basis in information theory and frequentist-based
inference. BIC is a similar concept.
• The AIC and BIC are both based on -2 times the maximized value of the
loglikelihood (-2LL); k is the number of parameters and LL is the log-likelihood
value at the MLE:
Here there is a
penalty for
AIC = 2k/n – 2LL/n, BIC = ln(n)*k – 2LL magnitude of the
LL; smaller is
better

• The score, as defined above, is minimized, e.g. the model with the lowest AIC is
selected.

AIC and BIC
• As you see from their definitions, AIC and BIC are similarly constructed,
with the essential difference being the term multiplied by k, the number
of estimated coefficients.
• As a result (2 vs. log(n)), the BIC tends to penalize model complexity more
heavily than the AIC.
• The AIC tries to select the model that most adequately describes an
unknown, high dimensional reality.
• Because the AIC is somewhat more permissive of model complexity than
the BIC, it may be preferred for prediction.
• When the intent is to create a model for explanation, BIC may be
preferred because it will tend to produce a simpler (i.e., more easily
interpretable) model.
• Importantly, the derivation of BIC under the Bayesian probability
framework means that if a selection of candidate models includes a true
model for the dataset, then the probability that BIC will select the true
model increases with the size of the training dataset. This cannot be said
for the AIC score.

Best Subset and
Computational Efficiency
• Best subset selection is very computationally demanding.
• For p = 30, for example, best subset will require fitting 230 = 1,073,741,824 models.
• Whereas forward selection (described next) will require fitting 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 + … +
29 + 30 = p(p + 1)/2 + 1 = 451 models.
• That said, the ease of fitting Gaussian linear models make even large (i.e., 30+
covariates) problems tractable within a few seconds. This is possible in part due to
advances in computational algorithms over the last decades.
• The problem of computational efficiency is quickly made worse, however, by using
other models that require iterative numerical methods for solving.
• For example, with Gaussian linear models (which can be solved analytically), a best
subset search with 11 predictors takes less than a second.
• By contrast, with a logistic model (solved using Newton’s method), 11 predictors
takes nearly 2 minutes, and adding each subsequent predictor causes the time to
more than double.

rowing teams (National Collegiate Athletic Association) within the Big Ten Conference
volunteered to participate in a study to predict race time for female collegiate rowers
from nineteen physical characteristics.

Multicollinearity - Variance Inflation Factors
• Problems when predictor variables are correlated among
themselves
– Regression Coefficients of predictors change, depending on
what other predictors are included
– Extra Sums of Squares of predictors change, depending on
what other predictors are included
– Standard Errors of Regression Coefficients increase when
predictors are highly correlated
– Individual Regression Coefficients are not significant, although
the overall model is
– Width of Confidence Intervals for Regression Coefficients
increases when predictors are highly correlated
– Point Estimates of Regression Coefficients are wrong sign (+/-)

Multicollinearity – Diagnostics
• Large changes in the estimated regression coefficients when a
predictor variable is added or deleted.
• Nonsignificant results in t-tests on the regression coefficients.

Multicollinearity Remedy –
Shrinkage Methods
Both the bias and the variance are desired to be low, as large values result in
poor predictions from the model. In fact, the model's error can be
decomposed into three parts: error resulting from a large variance, error
resulting from significant bias, and the remainder - the unexplainable part.

Ridge Regression
• Ridge regression is a way to create a parsimonious model when the number
of predictor variables exceeds the number of observations, or when a data
set has multicollinearity.
• The essential idea with ridge regression is identical to OLS regression with
one exception, there is a penalty added in the form of the l norm of the

coefficients.

Issues in Autocorrelated Data
One of the standard assumptions in the regression model is that the
error terms ε and ε are uncorrelated for i ≠ j. When error terms are
i j
correlated, problems occur when using OLS estimates:
– Regression Coefficients are still unbiased
– MSE underestimates 2
– Standard errors of regression coefficients based on OLS
underestimate the true standard error
– Inflated t and F statistics and artificially narrow confidence
intervals
Autocorrelated errors of 1st order:
ε = ρ ε + u
t t-1 t
where u are uncorrelated disturbances assumed normally
t
distributed.

• Ordinary Least Squares must be performed with correct variances

𝜂𝑡 𝑥 −𝜓 𝜂

In the above, 𝜂 is known as the natural parameter.

Definition
In either of the forms:
𝑡 𝑥 𝑞(𝜃)
𝑓 𝑥; 𝜃 = 𝑐 𝜃 ℎ 𝑥 𝑒
or
𝜂𝑡 𝑥 −𝜓 𝜂
𝑓 𝑥; 𝜂 = ℎ 𝑥 𝑒
t(X) is called the natural sufficient statistic.
• Sufficiency attempts to formalize the notion of no loss of
information.
• A sufficient statistic is supposed to contain by itself all the
information about the unknown parameters of the underlying
distribution that the entire sample could have provided.
• In that sense, there is nothing to lose by restricting attention
to just a sufficient statistic in one’s inference process.

Notice that d(y, μ) = 0 only when y = μ and otherwise, d(y, μ) > 0. In

shows that d(y, μ) can be interpreted as a type of distance measure
between y and μ.

Problems with Linear Regression
• Predicted values from linear regression are not constrained to
the interval [0,1], even though probabilities should be.
• Residuals from the linear model are not normally distributed
because they are dichotomous conditional X = x:
−𝑝 𝑥 if 𝑌 = 0
𝑖
𝜀 = ቊ
𝑖
1 − 𝑝 𝑥 if 𝑌 = 1
𝑖
• Also, the variance of ε is p(x)[1 – p(x)], which means it is
i
not constant.
• We need a method that deals with the above-mentioned
deficiencies.

Logistic/Sigmoid
Function
The sigmoid function
resembles an S-shaped curve.
It takes the real numbered
input values and converts
them between 0 to L (by
shrinking from both sides,
i.e., the negative values to 0
and very high positive ones
to L). Note that it can be
decreasing as well.

Other Response Functions
• A sigmoid function is a function having a
characteristic "S"-shaped curve or sigmoid curve.
• The logistic function is a prime candidate:
• An alternative is the probit function:
Φ 𝑡
where  is the cdf of the standard normal distribution.
Probit has tails that die off faster, which can behave
differently with rare events compared to the logistic
distribution.
• Theoretically, any cumulative distribution function
can serve as a response function.

Example: Bank Marketing
Data in 15-bank-full.csv come from here:
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/222/bank+marketing
• The data are related with direct marketing campaigns of a Portuguese
banking institution. The marketing campaigns were based on phone
calls. Often, more than one contact to the same client was required, to
access if the product (bank term deposit) would be ('yes') or not ('no')
subscribed.
• Explanatory variable thought possibly to affect the outcome were:
• Job occupation: admin.’, 'blue-collar’, 'entrepreneur’, 'housemaid’,
'management’, 'retired’, 'selfemployed’, 'services’, 'student’, 'technician’,
'unemployed’, 'unknown',
• Housing: has housing loan or no,
• Balance: average annual balance
• Marital status
• …
The data has 45,211 rows.

Logistic Regression Example
Use “glm” function to fit GLM models. “family” option determines the
distribution to be used. Choices are:
• binomial(link = "logit")
• gaussian(link = "identity")
• Gamma(link = "inverse")
• inverse.gaussian(link = "1/mu^2")
• poisson(link = "log")
• quasi(link = "identity", variance = "constant")
• quasibinomial(link = "logit")
• quasipoisson(link = "log")
See R code

Logistic Regression Example: Some Results
> logit0 = glm(y ~ balance, data = bank, family = "binomial")
> summary(logit0)
Coefficients:
Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) -2.081e+00 1.595e-02 -130.50 <2e-16 ***
balance 3.958e-05 3.840e-06 10.31 <2e-16 ***
Equation: log(odds of subsrcibing) = -2.081 + 0.00003958*balance
Interpretation of the slope:
• If the balance increases by 1 euro, then the log of the odds (of
subscribing) will increase by 0.00003958 units.
• Equivalent (and better!): the odds of subscribing will be multiplied
by e0.00003958 = 1.00004 for each extra 1 euro of balance.
• Final interpretation (resale): For each extra 1000 euros of balance
the odds of obtaining the product increase by 4%

• Unlike the closed-form analytical solution (i.e., the normal equations)
available for linear regression, the score function for logistic regression is
transcendental. That is, there is no closed-form solution so we must solve
with numerical optimization methods such as Newton’s method (IRLS)
which is done in several iterations.

(and score) function generalize directly to the case of p predictors.

Logistic Regression: Model Selection
• In multiple linear regression there were many selection criteria
available: R2, R2-adjusted, AIC, BIC, …
• R2 is based on the underlying assumption that you are fitting a linear
model. If you aren’t fitting a linear model, you shouldn’t use it!
• Only AIC and BIC can be adapted to the GLM with the same
formula but now using the logistic likelihood:
AIC = -2log L + 2p
BIC = -2log L + log(n) p
• Rule is the same: smaller AIC or BIC means better model.
• Note: regsubsets function from the leaps package is
applicable only for linear regression with normal errors. For GLM
you must use bestglm function from the bestglm package.
• See R code for the healthcare example

Introduction
Recall: Logistic regression model is applicable when the response
variable Y is dichotomous (that is, Y takes only two values).
Now: Response variable Y represents counts (that is, takes only whole
integer values).
Summary of Generalized Linear Models (GLM):
• Regular linear regression - when Y is continuous numerical.
• Logistic regression - when Y is categorical with only two outcomes.
• Poisson regression - when Y is a count.
• Multinomial or ordinal regression when Y is categorical with more
than two outcomes.
• Gamma regression – when the outcome variable is positive, like
amount of an insurance claim.

Poisson Data Examples
• Number of patients in an emergency room at any given time.
Possible predictors: time, day of the week, season, …
• Number of awards won by a movie. Predictors include number of
stars in the movie, budget, IMDB rating, …
• Number of children per family, based on income, race/ethnicity,
place of residence (urban or rural), …

Assumptions
• Observations are independent (standard statistical assumption except
in time series analysis and Markov chains)
• The distribution of Y (conditional on the X variables) follows a
Poisson distribution:
𝑌|𝑿 ~ 𝑃𝑜𝑖𝑠𝑠𝑜𝑛 𝜆 𝑿
Recall that 𝜆 > 0 is a parameter of the distribution equal to both the
mean and the variance
• Because of the properties of the Poisson distribution, the Poisson
regression model is known to satisfy the equidispersion property. If
data indicate overdispersion (variance greater than the mean), then
alternatives like the negative binomial regression might be more
appropriate.

Poisson Regression Model
• Log-Linear model:
log[E(Y|X)] = log(𝜆) = b + b X + … + b X
0 1 1 p p
or equivalently
E(Y|X) = 𝜆 = exp(b + b X + … + b X )
0 1 1 p p
• Like the logistic regression, exp(b ) represents the multiplicative
j
effect of the jth predictor on the mean, keeping the other predictors
fixed. That is, increasing X by one unit multiplies the mean of the
j
response variable by a factor of exp(b ).
j
• Empirical evidence shows that with count data the effects of
predictors are often multiplicative rather than additive. That is, one
typically observes small effects for small counts, and large effects
for large counts.

Alternatives
Common violations of Poisson assumptions include
• Overdispersion (more variance than predicted by the Poisson)
• Zero inflation (too many zeros)
• Quasipoisson & negative binomial are usually good options
for modeling overdispersion.
• In quasipoisson we assume 𝑉𝑎𝑟 = 𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜇 = 𝜙𝜇
• Mixture models can be used to model “zero inflated” data.

• D6 = indicator for Northwest other (ref: California Chardonnay
• D7 = indicator for French (ref: California Chardonnay)

Interpretation of the Poisson Regression parameters
• Coefficient for X (the price) = -0.084:
for each extra $1 increase in price, log[E(Y)] decreases by 0.084 units,
keeping other predictors fixed. That is, expected quantity sold will
multiply by exp(-0.084) = 0.92. This means there is 8% decrease in
demand for every $1 increase in price (as per basic economics theory).
• Coefficient for D1 (lowest price in its category), that is -0.912, with
exp(-0.912) = 0.4017119:
there is 59.8% decrease in demand if wine is the cheapest in its
category and all other predictors are the same.
• Which variety is the most popular?
D3-D7 are all negative, so when any of D3-D7 are equal to 1, demand
decreases, meaning reference region California Chardonnay has the
highest demand.
• What is the effect on demand if wine is available by the glass?
It increases demand as D2 has coefficient 1.755

Is the equidispersion assumption satisfied?
• Sample mean of Y is 1.38, while sample variance is 7.55, so it looks
like assumption is violated. However, we need to adjust for the
predictors.
After adjusting for the predictor variables overdispersion drops
to 1.148339 (not in base R output, but there is a package for
overdispersion test). This still indicates of a problem – should be
equal to 1!

More Complicated GLMs
•
We studied Gaussian, logistic and Poisson GLMs.
•
Many datasets include dependent variables whose distributions cannot be represented by
the normal, binomial or Poisson distributions.
•
For example, distributions of common survey questions such as “How much do you like
this class? (1–7)”.
•
Sometimes numerical variables, like age and years of education are binned into ordered
categories, the number of which may vary from survey to survey.
•
In such situations, sometimes the research interest lies not in the scale of each individual
variable, but rather in the associations between the variables.
•
Even if the variables are not artificially binned, it is natural to think of many ordinal,
non-numeric variables as arising from some underlying latent numeric process.
•
For example, the severity of a disease might be described “low”, “moderate” or “high”,
although we imagine a patient’s actual condition lies within a continuum.
•
There are specialized, complex distribution for ordered categories, ranks or nominal data.

Ordinal Regression
Ordinal logistic regression is a statistical analysis method that can be
used to model the relationship between an ordinal response variable
and one or more explanatory variables.
The model is:
𝑃 𝑌 ≤ 𝑗
log = 𝛽 − 𝛽 𝑥 − 𝛽 𝑥 − ⋯ − 𝛽 𝑥 , 𝑗 = 1, … , 𝐽 − 1
𝑗0 1 1 2 2 𝑝 𝑝
1 − 𝑃 𝑌 ≤ 𝑗
The model incorporates a negative sign so that there is a direct
correspondence between the slope and the ranking. Thus, a positive
coefficient indicates that as the value of the explanatory variable
increases, the likelihood of a higher-ranking increases.

Ordinal Regression Example
Suppose we are interested in describing the relationship between
the educational attainment and number of children of individuals
in a population. Additionally, we might suspect that an
individual’s educational attainment may be influenced by their
parent’s education level. The 1994 General Social Survey
provides data on variables DEG, CHILD and PDEG for a sample
of individuals in the United States, where DEG indicates the
i
highest degree obtained by individual i, CHILD is their number
i
of children and PDEG is the binary indicator of whether either
i
parent of i obtained a college degree.

Survival Analysis
• Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome

Survival Analysis
• Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome
variable: the time until an event occurs.
• For example, suppose that we have conducted a five-year
medical study, in which patients have been treated for
some disease.
• We would like to fit a model to predict patient survival
time, using features such as baseline health measurements
or type of treatment.
1/ 53

Survival Analysis
• Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome
variable: the time until an event occurs.
• For example, suppose that we have conducted a five-year
medical study, in which patients have been treated for
cancer.
• We would like to fit a model to predict patient survival
time, using features such as baseline health measurements
or type of treatment.
• Sounds like a regression problem. But there is an important
complication: some of the patients have survived until the
end of the study. Such a patient’s survival time is said to
be censored.
1/ 53

Survival Analysis
• Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome
variable: the time until an event occurs.
• For example, suppose that we have conducted a five-year
medical study, in which patients have been treated for
cancer.
• We would like to fit a model to predict patient survival
time, using features such as baseline health measurements or
type of treatment.
• Sounds like a regression problem. But there is an important
complication: some of the patients have survived until the
end of the study. Such a patient’s survival time is said to
be censored.
• We do not want to discard this subset of surviving
patients, since the fact that they survived at least five
years amounts to valuable information.
1/ 53

Non-medical Examples
3/ 53 Non-medical Examples
• The applications of survival analysis extend far beyond
medicine. For example, consider a company that wishes to
model churn, the event when customers cancel subscription
to a service.
3/ 53

Non-medical Examples
• The applications of survival analysis extend far beyond
medicine. For example, consider a company that wishes to
model churn, the event when customers cancel subscription
to a service.
• The company might collect data on customers over some
time period, in order to predict each customer’s time to
cancellation.
• However, presumably not all customers will have cancelled
their subscription by the end of this period; for such
customers, the time to cancellation is censored.
3/ 53

Non-medical Examples
• The applications of survival analysis extend far beyond
medicine. For example, consider a company that wishes to
model churn, the event when customers cancel subscription
to a service.
• The company might collect data on customers over some
time period, in order to predict each customer’s time to
cancellation.
• However, presumably not all customers will have cancelled
their subscription by the end of this time period; for such
customers, the time to cancellation is censored.
• Survival analysis is a very well-studied topic within
statistics. However, it has received relatively little
attention in the machine learning community.
3/ 53

Survival and Censoring Times
4/ 53 Survival and Censoring Times
• For each individual, we suppose that there is a true failure
or event time T, as well as a true censoring time C.
4/ 53

Survival and Censoring Times
• For everyone, we suppose that there is a true failure
or event time T, as well as a true censoring time C.
• The survival time represents the time at which the event of
interest occurs (such as death).
• By contrast, the censoring is the time at which censoring
occurs. For example, the time at which the patient drops
out of the study, or the study ends.
4/ 53

Survival and Censoring Times — Continued
5/ 53 Survival and Censoring Times — Continued
• We observe either the survival time T or else the censoring
time C. Specifically, we observe the random variable
Y = min(T,C).
5/ 53

Survival and Censoring Times — Continued
• We observe either the survival time T or else the censoring
time C. Specifically, we observe the random variable
Y = min(T,C).
• If the event occurs before censoring (i.e. T < C) then we
observe the true survival time T; if censoring occurs before
the event (T > C) then we observe the censoring time. We
also observe a status indicator,
1 if T ≤ C
𝛿 = ቐ
0 if T > C
Finally, in our dataset we observe n pairs (Y, δ), which we
denote as (y1, δ1),..., (yn, δn).

A Closer Look at Censoring
7/ 53 A Closer Look at Censoring
• Suppose that a few patients drop out of a cancer
study early because they are very sick.
7/ 53

A Closer Look at Censoring
• Suppose that a number of patients drop out of a cancer
study early because they are very sick.
• An analysis that does not take into consideration the
reason why the patients dropped out will likely
overestimate the true average survival time.
• Similarly, suppose that males who are very sick are more
likely to drop out of the study than females who are very
sick. Then a comparison of male and female survival times
may wrongly suggest that males survive longer than
females.
7/ 53

A Closer Look at Censoring
• Suppose that a number of patients drop out of a cancer
study early because they are very sick.
• An analysis that does not take into consideration the
reason why the patients dropped out will likely
overestimate the true average survival time.
• Similarly, suppose that males who are very sick are more
likely to drop out of the study than females who are very
sick. Then a comparison of male and female survival times
may wrongly suggest that males survive longer than
females.
• In general, we need to assume that, conditional on the
features, the event time T is independent of the censoring
time C. The two examples above violate the assumption of
independent censoring.
7/ 53

The Survival Curve
• Recall: Cumulative distribution (CDF):
• Probability event before-or-at time x
• Now: Complementary cumulative distribution
• (CCDF): Probability not-event-yet
• Example: Exponential distribution:

The Survival Curve
• The survival function (or curve) is defined as
S(t) = 1– F(t) = Pr(T > t)
8/ 53

The Survival Curve
• The survival function (or curve) is defined as
S(t) = Pr(T > t).
• This decreasing function quantifies the probability of
surviving past time t.
• For example, suppose that a company is interested in
modeling customer churn. Let T represent the time that a
customer cancels a subscription to the company’s service.
8/ 53

The Survival Curve
• The survival function (or curve) is defined as
S(t) = Pr(T > t).
• This decreasing function quantifies the probability of
surviving past time t.
• For example, suppose that a company is interested in
modeling customer churn. Let T represent the time that a
customer cancels a subscription to the company’s service.
• Then S(t) represents the probability that a customer
cancels later than time t. The larger the value of S(t), the
less likely that the customer will cancel before time t.
8/ 53

Estimating the Survival Curve
Ecologists classify species into three distinct categories based on their
probability of survival. The key differences between these three patterns
are related to the stage at which the probability of survival is greatest.
Type I species have high probability of survival throughout life with a
rapid decline late in life while Type II species are the opposite with a
rapid decline early in life and a high probability of survival late in life.
9 / 53

survival times for patients with primary brain tumors
undergoing treatment with stereotactic radiation methods.
• The predictors are gtv (gross tumor volume, in cubic
centimeters); sex (male or female); diagnosis (meningioma,
LG glioma, HG glioma, or other); loc (the tumor location:
either infratentorial or supratentorial); ki (Karnofsky
index); and stereo (stereotactic method).

probability that a patient survives for more than 20
months,
• It is tempting to simply compute the proportion of patients
who are known to have survived past 20 months, that is,
the proportion of patients for whom Y > 20.
• This turns out to be 48/88, or approximately 55%.
• However, this does not seem quite right: 17 of the 40
patients who did not survive to 20 months were censored,
and this analysis implicitly assumes they died before 20
months. Hence it is probably an underestimate.
10/ 53

Estimating the Survival Curve — Continued
• Alternatively, to estimate S(20), we could consider computing
the proportion of patients for whom Y > 20, out of the 71
patients who were not censored by time t = 20; this comes out
to 48/71, or approximately 68%.
• However, this is not quite right either, since it amounts to
completely ignoring the patients who were censored before
time t = 20, even though the time at which they are censored is
potentially informative. For instance, a patient who was
censored at time t = 19.9 likely would have survived past t =
20 had he or she not been censored.

The Log-Rank Test — Continued

choice: but the presence of censoring again creates a
complication.

The Log-Rank Test — Continued
• Females seem to fare a little better up to about 50 months,
but then the two curves both level off to about 50%. How
can we carry out a formal test of equality of the two
survival curves?
• At first glance, a two-sample t-test seems like an obvious
choice: but the presence of censoring again creates a
complication.

Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset
25/ 53 Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset
• Comparing the survival times of females and males on the
BrainCancer data gives a log-rank test statistic of W = 1.2,
which corresponds to a two-sided p-value of 0.2.
25/ 53

Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset
• Comparing the survival times of females and males on the
BrainCancer data gives a log-rank test statistic of W = 1.2,
which corresponds to a two-sided p-value of 0.2.
• Thus, we cannot reject the null hypothesis of no difference
in survival curves between females and males.
25/ 53

Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset
• Comparing the survival times of females and males on the
BrainCancer data gives a log-rank test statistic of W = 1.2,
which corresponds to a two-sided p-value of 0.2.
• Thus, we cannot reject the null hypothesis of no difference
in survival curves between females and males.
• The log-rank test is closely related to Cox’s proportional
hazards model, which we discuss next.

Regression Models with a Survival Response
• We now consider the task of fitting a regression model to
survival data.
• We wish to predict the true survival time T.
Since the observed quantity Y = min(T,C) is positive and
may have a long right tail, we might be tempted to fit a
linear regression of log(Y) on X. But censoring again
creates a problem.
• To overcome this difficulty, we instead make use of a
sequential construction, similar to the idea used for the
Kaplan-Meier survival curve.

Proportional Hazards Model— Continued
• What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h0(t)
is unspecified?
• Basically, we make no assumptions about its functional
form. We allow the instantaneous probability of death at
time t, given that one has survived at least until time t, to
take any form.
30/ 53

Proportional Hazards Model— Continued
• What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h0(t)
is unspecified?
• Basically, we make no assumptions about its functional
form. We allow the instantaneous probability of death at
time t, given that one has survived at least until time t, to
take any form.
• This means that the hazard function is very flexible and
can model a wide range of relationships between the
covariates and survival time.
30/ 53

Proportional Hazards Model— Continued
• What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h0(t)
is unspecified?
• Basically, we make no assumptions about its functional
form. We allow the instantaneous probability of death at
time t, given that one has survived at least until time t, to
take any form.
• This means that the hazard function is very flexible and
can model a wide range of relationships between the
covariates and survival time.
• Our only assumption is that a one-unit increase in xij
corresponds to an increase in h(t|xi) by a factor of exp(βj).

Partial Likelihood — Computation
40/ 53 Partial Likelihood — Computation
• To estimate β, we simply maximize the partial likelihood
with respect to β. As is the case for logistic regression, no
closed-form solution is available, and so iterative
algorithms are required.
40/ 53

Partial Likelihood — Computation
• To estimate β, we simply maximize the partial likelihood
with respect to β. As is the case for logistic regression, no
closed-form solution is available, and so iterative
algorithms are required.
• In addition to estimating β, we can also obtain other model
outputs, like those in least squares regression and logistic
regression.
• For example, we can obtain p-values corresponding to
particular null hypotheses (e.g. H0 : βj = 0), as well as
estimated standard errors and confidence intervals
associated with the coefficients.
40/ 53

Connection with the Log-Rank Test
41/ 53 Connection with the Log-Rank Test
• Suppose that we have just a single predictor (p = 1) with
xi ∈{0,1}. To test whether there is a difference between
the survival times of the observations in the two groups, we
can consider taking two possible approaches:
41/ 53

Connection with the Log-Rank Test
• Suppose that we have just a single predictor (p = 1) with
xi ∈{0,1}. To test whether there is a difference between
the survival times of the observations in the two groups, we
can consider taking two possible approaches:
1. Fit a Cox proportional hazards model, and test the null
hypothesis H0 : β = 0. (Since p= 1, β is a scalar.)
41/ 53

Connection with the Log-Rank Test
• Suppose that we have just a single predictor (p = 1) with
xi ∈{0,1}. To test whether there is a difference between
the survival times of the observations in the two groups, we
can consider taking two possible approaches:
1. Fit a Cox proportional hazards model, and test the null
hypothesis H0 : β = 0. (Since p= 1, β is a scalar.)
2. Perform a log-rank test to compare the two groups.
41/ 53

Connection with the Log-Rank Test
• Suppose that we have just a single predictor (p = 1) with
xi ∈{0,1}. To test whether there is a difference between
the survival times of the observations in the two groups, we
can consider taking two possible approaches:
1. Fit a Cox proportional hazards model and test the null
hypothesis H0 : β = 0. (Since p= 1, β is a scalar.)
2. Perform a log-rank test to compare the two groups.
• Now when taking approach #1, there are several
possible ways to test H0. One way is known as a score test.
• It turns out that in the case of a single binary covariate,
the score test for H0 : β = 0 in Cox’s proportional hazards
model is exactly equal to the log-rank test.

The Proportional Hazards Model— Additional Details
The discussion of the proportional hazards model glossed over a
few subtleties:
• There is no intercept in the proportional hazards model

The Proportional Hazards Model— Additional Details
The discussion of the proportional hazards model glossed over a
few subtleties:
• There is no intercept in the proportional hazards model
because an intercept can be absorbed into the baseline
hazard h0(t).
• We have assumed that there are no tied failure times. In
the case of ties, the exact form of the partial likelihood is
more complicated, and a number of computational
approximations must be used.
42/ 53

The Proportional Hazards Model— Additional Details
The discussion of the proportional hazards model glossed over a
few subtleties:
• There is no intercept in the proportional hazards model
because an intercept can be absorbed into the baseline
hazard h0(t).
• We have assumed that there are no tied failure times. In
the case of ties, the exact form of the partial likelihood is
more complicated, and a number of computational
approximations must be used.
• The partial likelihood gets its name because it is not exactly
a likelihood. However, it is a very good approximation.
42/ 53

The Proportional Hazards Model— Additional Details
The discussion of the proportional hazards model glossed over a
few subtleties:
• There is no intercept in the proportional hazards model
because an intercept can be absorbed into the baseline
hazard h0(t).
• We have assumed that there are no tied failure times. In
the case of ties, the exact form of the partial likelihood is
more complicated, and a number of computational
approximations must be used.
• The partial likelihood gets its name because it is not exactly
a likelihood. However, it is a very good approximation.
• We have focused only on estimation of the coefficients β.
However, we may also wish to estimate the baseline hazard
h0(t), for instance so that we can estimate the survival
curve S(t|x). These are implemented in the survival
package in R.

STAT 5703
Statistical Inference and Modeling
Dobrin Marchev Motivation
• Until now, all models assumed i.i.d.
random sample framework
• We now consider a dependence structure
between the observations X , … , X
1 n
• This can be done with two approaches:
Markov chains or time series
• Markov models provide a very rich set of
tools for handling dependence
Prof. Andrei A. Markov (1856-1922) , published his
result in 1906.
Markov later used his theory to study the distribution
of vowels in Onegin, written by Pushkin.

Data Science Example
• A common application of Markov chains in data science is
text prediction.
• It’s an area of NLP (Natural Language Processing) that is
commonly used in the tech industry by companies like
Google, LinkedIn and Instagram. When you’re writing
emails, Google predicts and suggests words or phrases to
autocomplete your email. And when you receive messages
on Instagram or LinkedIn, those apps suggest potential
replies.
• These are not the applications of a Markov chain we will
explore. The types of models these large-scale companies
use in production for these features are more
complicated. We will stick to simpler examples.

Example: the taxi problem
A taxi company has divided the city into three regions –
Northside, Downtown, and Southside. By keeping track of
pickups and drop-offs, the company has found that:
• Of the fares picked up in Northside, 50% stay in that region,
20% are taken to Downtown, and 30% go to Southside.
• Of the fares picked up Downtown, only 10% go to
Northside, 40% stay in Downtown, and 50% go to
Southside.
• Of the fares picked up in Southside, 30% go to each of
Northside and Downtown, while 40% stay in Southside.
We would like to know what the distribution of taxis will be
over time as they pick up and drop off successive fares.
Suppose we want to know the probability that a taxi starting
off Downtown, will be Downtown after letting off its seventh
fare?

State Diagram
This information can be
represented in a state
diagram which includes
• the three states D, N, and S
corresponding to the three
regions of the city
• the probabilities of a taxi
transitioning from one
region/state to another

Stationary Distribution
To understand the long-term behavior of our Markov chain, we introduce the
concept of a stationary distribution.
Definition: A Markov chain with transition matrix P is said to have a stationary
distribution 𝝅 (aka equilibrium or invariant distribution) if there exists a vector
𝝅 such that 𝝅′𝑷 = 𝝅′
This equation implies that multiplying the equilibrium vector by the transition
matrix P yields the same vector. In Markov chains, this is called "Pisa state" and
it refers to a specific kind of state where the chain can only reach from other
states but cannot transition away from it once entered.
Notice this means that 𝝅 must be a left eigenvector, corresponding to
eigenvalue 1. Note also that all transition matrices posses a right eigenvector,
corresponding to eigenvalue 1, since 𝑷𝟏 = 𝟏 , where 𝟏 is a vector of S 1’s.

Extensions
The state space S can in countably infinite. Then the transition
“matrix” is infinite as well! The theoretical results are a lot more
complicated.
A famous example is known as random walk.
For example, a random walk on the integer numbers ℤ can be
defined as being at position z and changing to either z + 1 or z – 1
with probabilities

Road ahead
• Markov chain theory is most widely used in the Markov chain
Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods in statistics.
• However, in MCMC the state space is usually uncountable, for
example, 𝑆 = (−∞, ∞), that is, 𝑋 ∈ 𝑆 would be a continuous
𝑛
random variable.
• The transition matrix becomes a transition kernel
𝑠 𝑠
(A matrix can be regarded as an operator from vectors in ℝ to ℝ ,
whereas the kernel transforms one density function to another
density function)
• The equilibrium distribution is not a vector but a pdf.
• The conditions for ergodicity are more complicated
• The eigenvectors are called eigenfunctions.
• There are infinitely many eigenvalues!

Columbia University STAT5703: Statistical Inference & Modeling Solutions for Homework 3 Professor: Dobrin Marchev TA: Satvik Saha March 8, 2026 1. # Load data df <- read.table("data/HW3-1.txt", header = TRUE) X <- df[, -1] # Extract covariates (a) (x.bar <- colMeans(X)) ## Sales Profit Assets ## 155.603 14.704 710.911 (S <- cov(X)) ## Sales Profit Assets ## Sales 7476.4532 303.61862 -35575.960 ## Profit 303.6186 26.19032 -1053.827 ## Assets -35575.9596 -1053.82739 237054.270 (b) # Extract (Sales, Profit) data X.SP <- X[, c("Sales", "Profit")] x.bar.SP <- colMeans(X.SP) S.SP <- cov(X.SP) S.SP.inv <- solve(S.SP) # Compute squared generalized distances d2.SP <- apply(X.SP, 1, function(x) { t(x - x.bar.SP) %*% S.SP.inv %*% (x - x.bar.SP) }) # Compute proportion of data falling within cutoff chi2.median <- qchisq(0.5, 2) mean(d2.SP <= chi2.median) ## [1] 0.4 This means that 40% of the data (based on sales, profit) falls within the 50% contour. (c) # Compute squared generalized distances S.inv <- solve(S) d2 <- apply(X, 1, function(x) { t(x - x.bar) %*% S.inv %*% (x - x.bar) }) n <- nrow(X) p <- ncol(X) Columbia University STAT5703: Statistical Inference & Modeling chi2.q <- qchisq((1:n - 0.5)/n, df = p) # Chi-squared quantiles # Plot chi-squared quantiles against d2 plot( sort(d2), chi2.q, xlim = c(min(d2) - 0.2, max(d2)), xlab = expression("Sorted squared distances " ~ d[(i)]^2), ylab = expression(chi[3]^2 ~ "quantiles"), main = expression(chi^2 ~ "Q-Q plot") ) text( sort(d2) - 0.1, chi2.q, df[order(d2), "Company"], adj = c(1, 0.5) ) abline(0, 1, col = "red") # Reference line (perfect alignment) 0 1 2 3 4 5 c 2 Q−Q plot Sorted squared distances d( 2 ) i selitnauq c ExxonMobil Citi BP Toyota INGGroup BoA Shell HSBC AIG GE The data for ExxonMobil lies quite far away from the red reference line, suggesting that it may Columbia University

2 sigma <- 1 SSE.diffs <- replicate(n.simulations, { # Generate training data X.train <- rnorm(n.train, 0, 5) Y.train <- beta.0 + beta.1 * X.train + rnorm(n.train, 0, sigma) df.train <- data.frame(X = X.train, Y = Y.train) # Fit models model.linear <- lm(Y ~ X, data = df.train) model.cubic <- lm(Y ~ X + I(X^2) + I(X^3), data = df.train) # Compute train SSEs SSE.linear.train <- sum(residuals(model.linear)^2) SSE.cubic.train <- sum(residuals(model.cubic)^2) # Generate testing data X.test <- rnorm(n.test, 0, 5) Y.test <- beta.0 + beta.1 * X.test + rnorm(n.test, 0, sigma) df.test <- data.frame(X = X.test, Y = Y.test) # Compute test SSEs SSE.linear.test <- sum((Y.test - predict(model.linear, newdata = df.test))^2) SSE.cubic.test <- sum((Y.test - predict(model.cubic, newdata = df.test))^2) # Keep SSE differences (cubic - linear) c( Columbia University STAT5703: Statistical Inference & Modeling SSE.cubic.train - SSE.linear.train, SSE.cubic.test - SSE.linear.test ) }) SSE.diffs <- as.data.frame(t(SSE.diffs)) colnames(SSE.diffs) <- c("Train", "Test") summary(SSE.diffs) # SSE.cubic - SSE.linear ## Train Test ## Min. :-1.461e+01 Min. : -9.98816 ## 1st Qu.:-2.727e+00 1st Qu.: -0.03427 ## Median :-1.387e+00 Median : 1.61874 ## Mean :-2.008e+00 Mean : 4.17529 ## 3rd Qu.:-6.169e-01 3rd Qu.: 4.73028 ## Max. :-8.715e-04 Max. :301.68417 mean(SSE.diffs[, "Test"] > 0) ## [1] 0.74 Note that the training SSE differences (cubic – linear) are always negative, as predicted in part (a). Consider again the data from class about rowing times. There were 90 college athletes competing in a 2000 m event. In this problem we want to understand how price affects the demand for cheese. In the data we used each row correspond to a week of sales for a particular Kroger store. There were 706 stores sampled in 11 cities. For each store three variables were recorded: city represents the city name of where the store is located, while the price and vol represent price (in $ per lb) and sales volume (in number of packages of cheese sold). We fit two linear regression models to predict the volume by the other variables. The following code was used to compute a test error rate. Is it resulting in a correct estimate? If not, what’s wrong? 1 # Given dataset of 1000-by-50 feature

If not, what’s wrong? 1 # Given dataset of 1000-by-50 feature 2 # matrix X, and 1000-by-1 labels vector 4 mu = np.mean(X, axis=0) 5 X = X - mu 7 idx = np.random.permutation(1000) 8 TRAIN = idx[0:900] 9 TEST = idx[900::] 11 ytrain = y[TRAIN] 12 Xtrain = X[TRAIN, :] 14 # solve for argmin_w ||Xtrain*w - ytrain||_2 15 w = np.linalg.solve(np.dot(Xtrain.T, Xtrain), np.dot(Xtrain.T, ytrain)) 17 b = np.mean(ytrain) 19 ytest = y[TEST] 20 Xtest = X[TEST, :] 22 train_error = np.dot(np.dot(Xtrain, w)+b - ytrain, 23 np.dot(Xtrain, w)+b - ytrain ) / len(TRAIN) 24 test_error = np.dot(np.dot(Xtest, w)+b - ytest, 25 np.dot(Xtest, w)+b - ytest ) / len(TEST) 27 print('Train error = ', train_error) 28 print('Test error = ', test_error) The error is at the beginning of the program on lines 4 and 5. Notice how µ is a function of both the train and test data. By de-meaning the entire dataset before splitting, we are intertwining the train and test data.
